text: Practice the '20-20-20' rule: Every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds.
Feeling overwhelmed? Try 5 deep breaths. It instantly activates your relaxation response.
Check your screen height: Eyes should be level with the top of the monitor to protect your neck.
After a big task, give yourself 5 minutes of 'digital silence'—no phone, no pings, just peace.
Stay hydrated! Even mild dehydration can lead to a drop in focus and a rise in irritability.
Batch your emails. Checking them only 3 times a day significantly reduces cognitive switching costs.
Practice self-compassion. Treat yourself with the same kindness you would offer a teammate.
Keep your elbows at a 90-degree angle while typing to prevent repetitive strain injuries.
Try the 5-4-3-2-1 grounding technique: Name 5 things you see, 4 you feel, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, and 1 you taste.
Expose yourself to natural light for at least 15 minutes to help regulate your circadian rhythm.
A quick 'thank you' or acknowledgement of a job well done is a simple way to contribute to a positive culture.
Use 'Brain Dumping': Write down all your pending tasks before leaving to prevent 'work-worry' at night.
Label your emotions. Just naming a feeling like 'anxiety' can reduce its intensity in the brain.
Don't cross your legs for long periods; it can affect your posture and blood circulation.
Listen to 60 seconds of 'Brown Noise' or rain sounds to quickly mask distracting office echoes.
Take a 30-second eye break by looking out a window or at a far wall between focus blocks.
Sharing a small, non-work recommendation—like a great podcast or a lunch spot—helps keep the mood light.
Single-task today. Focusing on one thing at a time is 40% more productive than multitasking.
Create a 'Transition Ritual' between work and home—like changing clothes or a short walk.
Adjust your chair's lumbar support. Your lower back should feel gently pushed forward.
Close your eyes for one minute and just follow your breath. It's a hard reset for your nervous system.
Swap your second coffee for a glass of cold water. Dehydration often masquerades as fatigue.
The Zeigarnik Effect: Unfinished tasks drain energy. Finish one small thing to clear mental space.
Accept that some days will be less productive than others. Progress isn't always a straight line.
Your feet should be flat on the floor. Use a footrest or a box if they don't reach comfortably.
Wash your hands with cold water. The cold sensation can snap you out of a stress loop.
Eat a protein-rich snack in the afternoon to avoid the '3 PM slump' caused by sugar crashes.
Constructive feedback can be a helpful tool for growth when centered on collaborative problem-solving.
Limit decision fatigue. Decide your most important task for tomorrow before you finish today.
It's okay to say 'I'll get back to you' to protect your current headspace and focus.
Reduce glare on your screen. If you see your reflection, the contrast is likely straining your eyes.
Try 'Box Breathing': Inhale for 4, hold for 4, exhale for 4, hold for 4. Repeat 4 times.
Take the stairs instead of the elevator once today. Small movements accumulate into health.
Apply the '2-Minute Rule': If a task takes less than 2 minutes, do it immediately to clear it.
Unsubscribe from one unnecessary notification source today to reduce digital clutter.
Roll your shoulders back and down every hour. We often carry stress in our traps unconsciously.
Spend 2 minutes observing a plant or looking at nature. It lowers cortisol levels instantly.
Caffeine has a 6-hour half-life. Try to have your last cup before 2 PM for better sleep.
Acknowledging a team win—even a very small one—can be a powerful buffer against daily stress.
Tidy your physical desktop. A cluttered space often leads to a cluttered mind.
Practice 'Gratitude Priming': Think of one thing you're glad for before your first meeting.
Keep your mouse close to your keyboard to avoid reaching, which can strain your shoulder.
Savor your first sip of water or tea. Notice the temperature and the feeling. That's mindfulness.
A 10-minute power nap can restore alertness better than a large coffee in the late afternoon.
Noticing and mentioning a job well done is a simple way to foster a culture of mutual respect.
Implementation Intentions: Say 'If [distraction] happens, then I will [action]' to stay focused.
Stop comparing your 'behind-the-scenes' to everyone else's 'highlight reel'.
Blink often! We blink 60% less when looking at screens, which causes dry, tired eyes.
Try 'Somatic Shaking': Shake your arms and legs for 30 seconds to release built-up tension.
Eat your lunch away from your desk. The change of scenery is as important as the food.
Use 'Done List' instead of just a 'To-Do List' to see how much you've actually achieved.
Schedule a 'Worry Time' (15 mins) to address anxieties, then park them for the rest of the day.
Avoid using your laptop on your lap. It forces your neck into a steep, harmful curve.
Notice 3 beautiful or interesting things on your commute or during your walk today.
Stow your phone in a drawer for 1 hour. Reclaiming your attention is an act of health.
A supportive word or a sincere 'can I help with that?' goes a long way when a peer is busy.
The 'Pomodoro' technique: Work for 25 mins, break for 5. It prevents mental fatigue.
Write down one thing that went well today and *why*. This trains your brain for optimism.
Ensure your monitor is exactly at arm's length from your face.
Stretch your neck gently: Ear to shoulder, hold for 15 seconds, switch sides.
Nuts and seeds are high in brain-boosting Omega-3s and provide steady energy.
Limit open browser tabs. Every open tab is a small pull on your attention.
Disconnecting is productive. Your best ideas often come when you are *not* working.
Every time you stand up, stretch your hip flexors for 20 seconds.
Listen to one song you love without doing anything else. Just the music.
If you're feeling sluggish, try humming or deep sighing to stimulate the vagus nerve.
Publicly acknowledging a contribution can be a great way to boost a teammate's morale.
Eat the frog! Do your most dreaded task first to free up emotional energy.
Perfectionism is the enemy of the good. Aim for 'Excellent but Done'.
Clean your screen. Dust and smudges make your eyes work harder to focus.
Take a 'Sense Break': Notice the smell of your coffee or the feel of the air.
Walking while thinking or during a 1-on-1 can stimulate divergent creativity.
Set a 'No-Meeting' block for your most complex work to protect your peak energy times.
Don't take slow replies personally; everyone is fighting their own battle with their inbox.
Try a vertical mouse if you feel wrist strain; it keeps your arm in a natural position.
Do a 2-minute 'Body Scan'. Where are you holding tension? Just notice it.
Your brain uses 20% of your total energy. Don't skip meals if you need to stay focused.
Take a mental 'snapshot' of a pleasant moment during the day to recall later when stressed.
The 'Power of Yet'. Instead of 'I can't do this', say 'I can't do this *yet*'.
Detach your worth from your productivity. You are valuable regardless of your output.
Keep your monitor directly in front of you. Turning your head to one side strains the neck.
Deep sighing: Inhale through the nose, then double exhale through the mouth to vent stress.
Blue light from screens inhibits melatonin. Dim your screen 2 hours before sleep.
Sharing knowledge or tips with others builds a stronger sense of purpose and team unity.
Limit multitasking. It can reduce your effective cognitive performance significantly.
Stop the scroll. Continuous scrolling activates the threat-detection center of your brain.
Check your chair height. Your knees should be level with or slightly lower than your hips.
Stare at the sky for 1 minute. The vastness helps put small problems into perspective.
Vitamin D is essential for mood. If it's sunny, sit by a window or step outside briefly.
Looking for shared objectives during a disagreement helps maintain a professional environment.
Set 'Implementation Intentions': 'I will do X at time Y'. This significantly increases success.
Practice 'Radical Acceptance'. Stop fighting things you cannot control to save your energy.
Neutral wrists! Your wrists should be flat, not angled up or down, while using your mouse.
Do a 60-second stretch: Reach for the ceiling, then touch your toes. Energize!
Assume positive intent in emails. Text has no tone, so choose the kindest interpretation.
